Revenue Growth - TTM

16.4%

Net Income Margin

-35.6%

EBITDA Margin

-37.3%

Market Capitalization

$990,560,000

Enterprise Value

$847,700,000

Operating Cash Flow

-$15,830,000

EPS (Fully Diluted)

-$1.46

Free Cash Flow per Share

-$0.37

Seeking Alpha Quant Score

Hold: 2.88

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

The company's Rule of 40 performance has produced improvement primarily due to a less negative operating margin offsetting a lower topline revenue growth rate:

Rule of 40 Performance (Unadjusted)

FQ3 2025

FQ1 2026

Revenue Growth %

19.3%

16.4%

Operating Margin

-37.2%

-28.8%

Total

-17.9%

-12.4%

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

Capital Allocation

Over the trailing twelve months, free cash used was $18.8 million, and capital expenditures were $3 million.

The company also paid a whopping $61.3 million in stock-based compensation in the last four quarters, so free cash flow net of SBC was negative ($81.1 million).

The net dollar increase in stock outstanding for the last four quarters was valued at $9.9 million.

SBC as a percentage of revenue over the trailing twelve-month period was 29.3%, a very high number for a mature company.

Compared to MongoDB’s recent 24.6% result, Couchbase is paying out significantly higher SBC as a percentage of revenue while having a higher CapEx burden and still generating negative free cash flow.

Management hasn’t recently disclosed any future plans for capital allocation in relation to its shares, so investors are in the dark as to this aspect of its financial operations.

Why I’m Neutral On Couchbase

Couchbase has shown a reduced topline revenue growth rate along with improved, though still negative, operating margin.

This has been a common theme among operating loss-making software companies since the sharp increase in the cost of capital has penalized them heavily.

However, they have followed a similar trajectory of essentially sacrificing topline revenue growth for better operating results, putting at risk their ‘growth’ label and valuation in the bargain.

The company’s growth strategy entails investing in its Capella DBaaS platform, both on-platform features and integrations with ecosystem partners, positioning its offerings for higher-value propositions, and expanding its routes to market.

The graphic below illustrates the frequency of various typically negative keywords in the firm’s most recent conference call:

The chart shows the company continues to face macroeconomic challenges as clients take longer to make final purchase decisions, resulting in longer sales cycles.

Couchbase is also facing customer count decline as developers have moved from its starter pack promotion over to its free tier, although management says that really is expanding the top of the funnel.

Regarding valuation, the market is valuing BASE at a forward EV/Sales multiple of around 3.7x on an estimated NTM revenue growth rate of 13.1% against a median Meritech Capital SaaS Index implied ARR growth rate of approximately 14% (Source).

Also, the benchmark index indicates a recent median Net Dollar Retention Rate of 110% vs. Couchbase’s NRR of 114%.

The index of publicly held SaaS application software companies showed a forward EV/Revenue multiple median of around 5.3x on May 30, 2025, as the chart shows here:

So, by comparison, Couchbase is currently being valued by investors at a discount to the broader index as of May 30, 2025.

I’ve also compared Couchbase to competitor MongoDB, with their major metrics shown in the table here:

Metric

MongoDB

Couchbase

Variance

EV/Sales - FWD

5.9

3.7

-37.5%

EV/EBITDA - FWD

57.4

NM

--%

Rev. Growth Est. - FWD

16.2%

13.1%

-18.9%

Net Income Margin

-6.4%

-35.6%

--%

FCF Yield Per Share - TTM

0.84%

-1.99%

--%

(Source - Seeking Alpha)

MDB is growing revenue at a faster growth rate, even though it is a much larger company and has positive EBITDA and free cash flow per share, so I’m not surprised it is garnering a higher valuation.

Also, the market’s EV/Sales multiple has dropped 20% in the past year, suggesting it is enamored with BASE’s growth prospects.

Looking ahead, management raised full-year guidance for total revenue and ARR and said it is aiming for profitability in fiscal 2027. If so, it has a long way to go to achieve GAAP operating breakeven, even with continued uptake on its Capella platform.

While operating losses are narrowing, they remain elevated. Given the company’s dilutive capital allocation, lack of visibility on capital needs, and a decelerating topline growth rate, I’m a neutral Hold on Couchbase for the near term.

data.title: CorMedix: Exceeding Expectations
data.description:
Today, I am putting CorMedix Inc. (NASDAQ:CRMD) in the spotlight for the second time here in 2025. When I first covered this small cap name, CorMedix was just entering full blown commercialization with its flagship product, an antimicrobial catheter lock solution called DefenCath. I noted this device has the potential to reduce catheter-related bloodstream infections in adult patients with kidney failure and become a 'best of breed' product in this niche market. Based on the numbers the company has put up since that article in the first week in January, both CorMedix and DefenCath have substantially exceeded initial expectations. As the result the stock has had a nice run here in 2025. Can the rally continue? An updated analysis follows below.

CorMedix, Inc. is headquartered in New Jersey. The company is focused on developing treatments for and devices to prevent infectious and inflammatory diseases. As noted before, the company's primary asset is DefenCath which comes in a vial of a patented blend of taurolidine, heparin, and citrate. This combination has compared favorably against heparin-based catheter lock solutions in reducing catheter-related bloodstream infections or CRBSIs. The product was fully approved just over a year ago. CorMedix's stock currently trades around $12.75 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $860 million.

DefenCath primary markets are the outpatient dialysis segment, which accounts for the lion's share of sales, as well as hospital inpatient settings. The product is the only FDA approved catheter lock solution that delivers significant risk reduction in CRBSIs.

The company is looking to expand the indications DefenCath is approved for. CorMedix recently initiated a Phase 3 study for the reduction of Central Line Associated Blood Stream Infections or CLABSIs for individuals receiving Total Parenteral Nutrition. Management is aiming for a NDA for this indication in late 2026 or early 2027. They believe this could add $150 million to $200 million annually to their potential market.

Recent Results:

The company posted Q1 numbers on May 6th. The firm delivered GAAP earnings of 30 cents a share, six cents a share above the consensus. CorMedix produced net income of $20.6 million and adjusted EBITDA of $23.6 million. Management did a good job of controlling expenses given the sales ramp up. Operating expenses rose 9% year-over-year to $17.4 million. Full year operating expenses are expected to come in between $72 million to $78 million in FY2025.

Revenues came in at $39.1 million, besting expectations by some $3 million. This compares to negligible sales in the same period a year ago and sequentially up from $31.2 million of revenues in Q4. Outpatient dialysis continues to account to the vast majority of sales. However, shipments to inpatient facilities more than doubled from a year ago to make up six percent of overall revenues. In addition, CorMedix's inpatient sales team is now fully staffed, trained and out in the field.

Management also noted that it now believes first half net sales will come in at the top end of its previous guidance of $62 million to $70 million. Leadership based this upwardly revised view on the latest order trends and inventory tracking with current purchasing customers. It also believes Q2 sales will come in near the top end of their previous guidance of $23 million to $31 million. Overall FY2025 sales will be impacted by the timing and scale of uptake of a large dialysis operator customer CorMedix recently signed.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

CorMedix is view positively by the analyst community. Since first quarter results hit the wires, four analyst firms including RBC Capital and Needham have reiterated Buy ratings on the stock. All four ratings contained minor upward price target revisions. Price targets range from $13 to $20 a share.

Just over 12% of the outstanding float in the shares is currently held short. There has been no insider activity in the stock so far in 2025. The company ended Q1 with $77.5 million in cash and marketable securities on the balance sheet after producing $19.7 million in operating cash flow during the quarter. CorMedix listed no long-term debt on the 10-K filed for Q1.

Conclusion:

CorMedix lost 30 cents a share (GAAP) on $43.5 million in revenues in FY2024. The current analyst firm consensus has CorMedix with 80 cents a share of profits in FY2025 on $144 million in sales. They project $1.51 a share of earnings in FY2026 on revenue growth of just over 50%.

That leaves the stock trading at roughly 16 times forward earnings and just over eight times FY2026E EPS. A bit cheaper if net cash on the balance sheet is taken into consideration. More than reasonable valuations even after a 55% gain so far in 2025.

The major risk around CorMedix is the company's complete reliance on DefenCath. If the product has any sort of issues in the future (Ex, reimbursement changes), it would be an existential risk to the company. While unlikely, that needs to be acknowledged. DefenCath does have 10 years of market exclusivity from its FDA approval it should be noted.

I have accumulated a fairly large stake in CRMD this year on the increasingly positive news flow. I have done so via covered call orders as liquidity in the options against this equity is solid and the option premiums are attractive. I will be adding to this stake as my previous covered call positions expire in the money in the months ahead. I will look for any decent dip in the stock to do so as CRMD seems like a solid long-term 'Accumulate' given the company's growth trajectory and current valuations.

data.title: Entravision Stock: Well-Positioned As Adtech Business Grows, And As FCC Eyes Deregulation
data.description:
Since the publication of my prior article on Entravision Communications (NYSE:EVC) last November, shares in the advertising technology and broadcasting company have pulled back to prior lows. This comes despite numerous positives for EVC stock emerging since the start of the year.

For one, there has been a key positive that has emerged for Entravision's legacy business. In the near future, the company may have the opportunity to capitalize on this development. Alongside this positive, the latest financials for EVC's newer, faster growing business suggest that this unit is worth materially more than I estimated it was worth in my prior write-up.

Although a breakout moment for EVC may not arrive immediately, as before, time is somewhat on your side. At least, given how Entravision continues to pay a very high dividend, adding a "paid while you wait" factor to this name.

With all of this in mind, let's dive in, take a closer look, and see why EVC remains a great opportunity among microcap value stocks.

EVC Stock: Recap of Recent Performance

Why has Entravision stock performed poorly over the past seven months? Chalk it up to the fading of election-year bullishness, plus the emergence of economic uncertainty in the wake of recent macro developments.

[object HTMLElement]

While not for certain, perhaps investors bid up EVC stock ahead of the 2024 U.S. Presidential Elections, due to how much Entravision benefits from the election cycle, given how it produces a short-term boost in ad demand and prices for the company's portfolio of Spanish-language television and radio stations.

In turn, once election day entered the rearview mirror, focus shifted back to other factors, such as the company's earnings release, interestingly enough held the day after election day. As discussed in my last write-up, Entravision delivered mixed results during Q3 2024, with revenue rising due to election year demand but earnings dropping due to one-time accounting items.

Following the fading of bullish momentum in November, shares were more-or-less rangebound until February, when EVC began to experience sharper price declines again. This is when the aforementioned "economic uncertainty" factor began to have a negative impact on shares.

Concerns about the impact of higher tariffs on the U.S. economy turned into concerns about advertising demand, further souring enthusiasm for EVC stock. However, while sentiment for EVC has continued to lean bearish, this could soon change. At least, assuming that the market begins to appreciate both recent favorable regulatory developments, as well as key takeaways from Entravision's latest quarterly earnings release.

FCC Catalyst Still on the Table

A key positive that has emerged since election day has been FCC leadership changes made by the new Trump administration. I did not touch on this factor in my last EVC stock write-up, but I did discuss it in a write-up on Sinclair (SBGI) back in April.

The FCC, under the leadership of Brendan Carr, has launched a massive deregulation initiative, codenamed "Delete, Delete, Delete." This initiative is viewed to be a prelude to the regulatory agency eliminating broadcast ownership rules regarded as being outdated in the digital era, such as the national TV station ownership cap, as well as caps on ownership of multiple stations in the same market.

If these regulations are eliminated, this in turn could spark the next big wave of media M&A. In this scenario, Entravision would be well-positioned to sell its radio and television stations to a larger competitor, possibly obtaining top dollar for these assets, due to the potential for strategic acquirers to benefit from cost synergies.

Alongside deregulation, the new FCC regime has publicly stated its interest in holding another broadcast spectrum auction. Entravision benefited greatly from the last spectrum auction held in 2017, generating a total of $263.6 million in gross proceeds.

Admittedly, it's unclear how soon, if at all, if the FCC's recent actions and announcements will result in a boon for Entravision. However, as mentioned above, this is not the only positive EVC has going for it.

Latest Earnings Release Provides Other Promising Takeaways

On May 8, Entravision released fiscal results for the quarter ending March 31, 2025. Although the legacy Media segment is mired in a post-election slump, with sales down 10% year-over-year, and operating income falling back into the red, factors such as macro uncertainty don't seem to be affecting EVC's Advertising Technology segment.

Last quarter, this segment reported 57% sales growth, as well as a nearly fourfold increase in operating profit. With this in mind, it's time to reassess how much this business is potentially worth. Later on, I'll provide my estimate for the value of Entravision's Advertising Technology segment.

Once again, one-time writedowns resulted in GAAP losses, but the reason behind these latest non-cash charges could be seen as yet another positive for EVC stock going forward. There were a total of $48.9 million in non-cash charges.

These charges were due primarily to the sale of two TV stations in Mexico, as well as due to Entravision's vacating of its previous corporate headquarters in Santa Monica, California.

Eliminating both non-core assets, as well as continuing to focus on cost-cutting measures, Entravision may be poised to report stronger results in the coming quarters, including perhaps a return to GAAP profitability.

On the Q1 2025 post-earnings conference call, Entravision's management provided further positive takeaways. As CEO Michael Christenson discussed on the call, the company reduced corporate expenses by $4.5 million during Q1 2025, or by around $18 million on an annualized basis.

As noted by CFO Mark Boelke on the call, the company expects operating expenses for the Advertising Technology business to "even out on an annualized basis with revenue increasing faster than expense to generate meaningful operating leverage in our Ad Tech & Services business."

EVC Stock Valuation

At current prices, Entravision Communications has a market cap of $181 million. Add to this figure $235.3 million in debt and lease liabilities, and subtract $78.2 million in cash, and we get an enterprise value of just over $338.1 million.

On a trailing twelve month (TTM) basis, EVC may look pricey, compared to other publicly-traded names in the space. Based on TTM EBITDA, Entravision trades at an EV/EBITDA ratio of 11x. This represents a valuation premium to that of peers like Sinclair, Gray Media (GTN), and E.W. Scripps (SSP), all of which trade at TTM EBITDA multiples in the 5x-6x range.

However, beyond the fact that these names are likely overlooked and undervalued as well, remember that Entravision is only partially a broadcasting company. Advertising Technology, made up of the company's Smadex and Adwake subsidiaries, generates a majority of Entravision's overall revenue. Last quarter, this segment also generated nearly 90% of segment-level EBITDA (segment operating profit plus depreciation and amortization).

In past coverage, I have provided a breakup value-based estimate for the underlying value of EVC stock. I will do so again, as seen below:

Media Segment (Entravision's Broadcasting Assets)

Last time we estimated the value of Entravision's broadcasting assets, we based it on the assumption of $28.5 million in annual EBITDA, based upon calculations that incorporated recent quarterly results, as well as estimated how much of Entravision's corporate overhead could be attributed to this segment.

This time, given the near-term slump of EVC's broadcasting business, I will use an alternative valuation, to assess how much these stations could be worth to a strategic buyer, especially if the FCC ultimately rescinds what remains of regulations that limit national and local TV and radio station ownership.

Last quarter, the Media segment reported revenue of just under $41 million. We will annualize this, to $164 million, even as it's possible that this segment reports much higher revenue in subsequent quarters. The EV/Sales ratios of Entravision radio and television broadcasting peers range widely, with names like radio station owner Sinclair trading at an EV/Sales ratio of 1.3, while Gray Media trades at an EV/Sales ratio of around 2.1.

However, erring on the side of conservatism, we will use a 1.5x sales multiple for this business. This provides us with a valuation of around $246 million, not that far off from the $228 million we estimated previously.

Advertising Technology (Smadex, Adwake)

Last quarter, Entravision's advertising technology segment generated $6.5 million in operating income. Add in depreciation, and quarterly EBITDA for this segment was around $7 million. This comes to around $28 million on an annualized basis.

Given management's remarks on the latest conference call, I feel confident to assume that Smadex and Adwake will generate even stronger results in the coming quarters.

Yes, you may be thinking, "what about corporate overhead expenses?" Good question. As before, I'll prorate corporate overhead. Last quarter, this segment represented around 55% of overall revenue. Corporate overhead last quarter also came in at around $7.8 million. With this, we'll shift $4.3 million in quarterly overhead, or around $17.2 million over to Advertising Technology.

Following this adjustment, Adtech is generating about $10.8 million in EBITDA on an annualized basis. Given how quickly this business is growing, I believe it should be valued at the upper-end of valuations of advertising stocks. Other programmatic advertising stocks like PubMatic (PUBM) and TechTarget (TTGT) are trading at EBITDA multiples in the high-teens to low-twenties.

Hence, assigning a 20x EBITDA multiple may be reasonable at this point. At this multiple, the Advertising Technology Segment is worth around $216 million, considerably more than the $137.6 million I estimated previously.

Adding it All Together

Adding these estimated values of the Media and Advertising Technology segments, we get an estimated gross valuation of around $444 million. Subtract $235.3 million in total debt/lease liabilities, and add back $78.2 million in cash, and we get a valuation of $286.9 million, or around $3.15 per share, a nearly 60% premium to the current trading price of EVC stock.

A Caveat and Two Silver Linings

While Entravision arguably trades at a discount to its underlying value, there is a caveat to keep in mind: realizing this value will take time, even as regulatory trends appear favorable. Again, it's unclear how soon the FCC could potentially de-regulate broadcast media, but presumably if it happens, it will happen between now and the next U.S. Presidential election.

In the meantime, if the lagging performance of Entravision's broadcast assets persists, overshadowing the further growth of the Advertising Technology segment, EVC could remain rangebound, or worse, fall again to lower prices.

That said, alongside this caveat are two silver linings. First, my valuation estimates may be conservative. I may be using an aggressive multiple to value the Adtech business based on current results, but if growth continues, this business could end up being worth far more than my $216 million current estimate.

The same goes for broadcasting, albeit not to an extreme of an extent. With Entravision cutting costs, as well as prioritizing content like news, as well as expanding its local digital advertising sales capabilities, the Media segment may be able to become materially more profitable, even in non-election years.

Don't forget, either, that Entravision could benefit from another spectrum auction. Even if not as significant of a windfall as the first spectrum auction, such an event would likely add a noticeable boost to potential upside.

Second, besides the fact I am conservatively-valuing Entravision's businesses, there may be not one, but two natural buyers for EVC's broadcasting stations, just waiting in the wings to scoop up these assets once deregulation happens.

For one, we have TelevisaUnivision, which controls Class U shares that give it a say in whether Entravision can be sold, or if it can sell its Univision and UniMás affiliates.

Although this dynamic suggests that TelevisaUnivision could stymie potential post-deregulation M&A, or even use these shares as leverage to acquire Entravision's stations at a favorable price, given how cheap EVC stock is today, a favorable price could still represent a windfall for the company.

Alongside TelevisaUnivision, there's also Estrella Media. Recently acquired by publicly-traded MediaCo Holding (MDIA), Estrella is another Spanish-language broadcast station owner. As of late, both Estrella and Entravision have been partnering up in various ways. Last fall, the two companies entered an ad-sales partnership for their respective radio station businesses. Moreover, as seen from Entravision's new corporate address: 1 Estrella Way, Burbank, CA.

If this means a merger between Entravision and MediaCo/Estrella is in the cards is anyone's guess. MediaCo has a market cap of just $61.2 million, and likely isn't in the position to snap up EVC. However, maybe this is the prelude to MediaCo/Estrella buying up EVC's radio stations. Or, perhaps the two companies may even be considering further partnerships. These in turn could help boost EVC's profitability and in turn its stock price.

The Bottom Line on EVC Stock

It may be frustrating that Entravision continues to be overlooked and undervalued, but in time, it could pay off in a big way, for those patient enough to hold onto a position.

In the years ahead, regulatory changes could enable EVC to sell off its legacy business at a favorable valuation. Another spectrum windfall may be in the cards as well. During this time frame, the company's Advertising Technology segment could continue growing at a rapid pace, becoming an even more valuable business in the process.

Worth at least $3 per share today, in time these assets could be worth an even greater premium to EVC's current share price. Not only that, as mentioned before, while waiting for Entravision's true value to be realized, investors benefit from EVC's high dividend. Still at 5 cents per share, this quarterly dividend gives the stock a 10.05% forward yield. Recent cost cutting, plus the Adtech segment's move to profitability, may increase the chances that this cash payout stays intact.

Taking all these factors into account, consider EVC stock still worthy of a buy, at present price levels and especially on any near-term weakness.

data.title: Joby's $1 Billion Saudi Deal: A Big Dream With Big Questions
data.description:
Joby Aviation, Inc. (NYSE:JOBY) announced just this week, on June 3rd, that they signed a memorandum of understanding (MoU) with Abdul Latif Jameel ("ALJ"), a massive business group in Saudi Arabia, to potentially deliver up to 200 of their electric aircraft. The total value of this partnership could go as far as $1 billion over the next few years. For the company that always brings zeros in sales, this is incredible, so no question, the market got really enthusiastic and sent the stock flying up.

The problem is that the MoU is not a legally binding contract. This is basically an agreement to collaborate and the discussion of some terms of this partnership, but without actually committing. And this is a very important part to understand. The fact that the company announced this memorandum does not mean it will get a cent from Saudis. This is how AI-powered Google search explains what a memorandum of understanding (MoU) is:

A memorandum of understanding (MOU) is a non-binding agreement between two or more parties that outlines a shared understanding of their roles, responsibilities, and objectives in a collaborative project or partnership. It's a formal document that serves to establish a common goal and a framework for cooperation, but it does not create legally enforceable obligations.

After everyone understands what we are dealing with, we can be a little excited about this memorandum. This is basically a handshake between Joby, which is one of the leading companies in the electric air taxi sector, and ALJ, which has a serious infrastructure and transport control across the Gulf region. So the potential is definitely here, but so are the unknowns.

Company Overview

Joby builds electric aircraft that take off and land vertically, which doesn't sound very impressive, because this is what helicopters do, but the technology of Joby is different. This aircraft is designed to be a flying taxi in cities, so it’s allegedly quiet, fast, and with zero emissions. I say “allegedly” only because I didn’t see one and didn’t experience it myself to know for sure. Moreover, I saw some claims that they might not be as quiet and not as nature-friendly as the company claims.

If all of the claims are real, I see a nice potential for this technology. I am not sure how big the market is gonna be, because currently, I could imagine Joby only in 2 cities I've been to - New York and Dubai, and I traveled a bit. These cities are the ones where people use helicopters, so for now, I see it as equivalent.

Now, there’s a reason for this Saudi news to get so much attention. Joby has been building and working on this technology for years. They want to create something big, and finally, we can say we see some progress. Besides, they’re passing the FAA. This is a certification that an aircraft is safe to use and to fly. Joby is currently at the end of the fourth stage of the aircraft design process, and it looks like they will be finishing soon. This could be a positive catalyst for the stock price, as this certificate is almost like FDA approval of a new drug.

They’ve also crossed a huge technical milestone. In April, they began flying full-scale aircraft with a pilot onboard through full transitions, from vertical takeoff into forward flight and back. According to Joby’s CEO JoeBen Bevirt, everything went exactly as they hoped it would:

The aircraft flew exactly as we expected with excellent handling qualities and low pilot workload. Since Buddy made the first transition to wingborne flight, two other Joby pilots have done the same and importantly, pilot-on-board transition flight has now become a routine part of our flight testing and we’re the first eVTOL company to be able to say that.

Joby is getting ready to bring that aircraft to Dubai for testing, and they plan to launch commercial operations also in Dubai by early 2026. If it happens, Joby will beat most of its eVTOL competitors, which are only in early development and testing stages.

The only problem in this bullish story is that the company hasn’t made any sales yet. Joby’s market cap is almost $7 billion. For the company that never earned anything. This is wild.

A Billion-Dollar Opportunity… or is it?

I want to discuss this Saudi deal more closely, but not as a guarantee that the company will start earning. This is a possibility that the company will start selling. Abdul Latif Jameel is one of the biggest conglomerates in the Middle East, and they have business in various industries, from Toyota car dealerships to renewable energy. Partnering with them is indeed a huge deal for the company because it opens the door to a whole region that is ready to throw millions into being seen as futuristic and innovative. I am from Israel, and our country is investing less in it, but Saudi Arabia and the UAE want their country to be the future, and they are succeeding in it. It may not look like it just yet, but believe me when I say, Dubai is transforming every two to three years to be more and more modern and technological. And of course, Joby’s taxi, which flies at a speed of over 200mph and has no emissions, aligns perfectly with this goal.

Management was excited long before the deal. On the Q1 earnings call, Joby’s Executive Chairman Paul Sciarra said they’re having “all the right conversations” about selling aircraft outside the U.S. and that their focus is on “credible customers and pre-orders that are as firm as possible.” I am curious if they meant this specific partnership or some other ones.

Anyway, the direction looks right and the future sounds promising. The company had different types of partnerships before, like Uber (UBER), Delta (DAL), and Toyota (TM), but no meaningful revenue has come from them. At least not until now, and investors had hoped it wouldn’t take so long.

Conclusion

Joby is one of those companies that trade on dreams about the future and promises that may or may not be fulfilled. As I already mentioned, it’s a $7 billion company with zero revenue. I don’t see any reason to look at any of the metrics we usually discuss, like the valuation, the profitability, or the growth. The company doesn’t have anything. This is not even a business yet, at least from my perspective. It’s a very impressive development that may or may not make some money in the future.

This technology has the potential to become truly revolutionary and to change the world. It also has a chance to become a more widely used helicopter in huge cities. As I said before, I see Joby’s taxis on Manhattan, for example. It would improve the city greatly, because the noise from all the copters is unbearable. Will or not this aircraft go further than that? Not for sure. And before you write me comments, I know that the market is supposed to become about $1 billion at some point. I know that Joby is cheaper, and not only the crowd that uses helicopters is supposed to use them.

But the key word in this is “supposed.” I am not playing a fortune teller. If you are fascinated by the idea, go ahead and buy the stock. The company has the money to sustain itself for about two years, and recently, we got some news about a major Toyota investment, which is also a big plus. If you are considering this stock, I would even think about buying a few similar companies just to have them. This is speculative, for sure. Not for value investors, for people who like the risk. The downside here is only 100%, but the upside could be seriously rewarding if it works.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Wedbush Launches AI-Focused ETF
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

The IVES ETF holds 30 companies identified by analyst Dan Ives. (0:15) Private sector shows anemic job growth. (0:57) UnitedHealth cancels former CEO stock awards. (3:50)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Wedbush star tech analyst Dan Ives, known for his bullish AI thesis, see his namesake exchange-traded-fund launch today.

The Dan Ives Wedbush AI Revolution ETF (NYSEARCA:IVES) – ticker symbol (surprise surprise) IVES – opened for trading at $25.37 today. The management fee is 0.75%.

“It's all about the use cases exploding which is driving this tech transformation being led by software and chips into the rest of 2025 and beyond and thus speaks to our tech bull and AI Revolution thesis with these 30 core names,” Ives said last month.

Among the 30 holdings are Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Nvidia (NVdA), Palantir (PLTR), Snowflake (SNOW), Apple (AAPL), Meta (META), Palo Alto Networks (PANW) AMD (AMD) and Micron (MU).

On the economic front, ADP said private sector employment rose by just 37,000 in May, missing the +110,000 consensus and cooling from 60,000 in April. That’s the lowest level since 2023.

That low figure caused some market jitters ahead of Friday’s official jobs report, wiping out gains in stock market futures and pushing the 10-year yield (US10Y) closer to 4.4%.

But as Pantheon Macro reminds us: “ADP’s forecast error, regardless of sign, has averaged 84K - and has been as big as 348K - since its methodology was overhauled in August 2022.”

“That's a bigger average error than for the consensus forecast, about 50K, and the 73K error from simply taking the average first official estimate of growth in private payrolls over the previous six months. The breakdown of ADP’s employment estimates is just as unreliable, with the average absolute forecast errors for every major sector also bigger than simply assuming the prior six-month average growth rate.”

But that didn’t stop President Donald Trump from highlighting ADP as another reason he thinks Fed Chairman Jay Powell should start cutting interest rates.

In addition, the May ISM services index painted what Schwab strategist Kathy Jones called a soft picture of the economy. The overall index fell into contraction territory at 49.9, down from 51.6 and below the 52 consensus. That’s the lowest level since in a year.

New orders slumped to 46.4 from 52.3, while prices paid rose to 68.7 vs. 65.1. Employment held above 50, though.

Among active stocks, Apple (AAPL) caught a rare downgrade from Needham, which cut the stock to Hold from Buy, amid concerns over earnings growth and iPhone competition.

Analyst Laura Martin said: “Despite AAPL's premium valuation, it is growing (revenues) and margins slowest among its Big Tech competitors. In fact, the other 3 Big Tech companies we cover reported 2x-3x faster rev growth and 3x-12x faster margin expansion than AAPL for the March quarter, suggesting that AAPL's premium valuation (versus) Big Tech peers is at risk.”

Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) is soaring after fiscal second-quarter results beat estimates.

Evercore analyst Amit Daryanani noted Hewlett Packard pointed to a more positive outlook than feared going into the print, guiding July-quarter revenue of $8.35 billion at midpoint, ahead of street at $8.22 billion and EPS of $0.40 to $0.45 versus consensus of $0.41. The company expects sequential growth across all of its product segments, including mid-teens quarter-over-quarter growth in servers, driven in part by the expected delivery of a large AI server order.

Guidewire Software (GWRE) is up after the digital platform provider for property and casualty insurance reported adjusted earnings per share of $0.88, which was much more than the consensus estimate of $0.47.

Looking ahead to Q4, Guidewire projects revenue between $332 million to $340 million, with a midpoint of $336 million more than the consensus of $332 million.

In other news of note, UnitedHealth Group (UNH) announced that board members canceled the performance-based restricted stock units granted to former CEO Andrew Witty, who abruptly resigned from the company in May.

Witty’s sudden departure sent UnitedHealth shares sharply lower in mid-May as the company concurrently withdrew its full-year earnings outlook under new CEO, Stephen Hemsley.

The Compensation and Human Resources Committee of the board decided to cancel Witty’s performance-based restricted stock units granted on Feb. 20. Witty has agreed to the cancellation, UnitedHealth added.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, Jefferies added six new names to its list of highest-conviction, Buy-rated stocks. The list comprises stocks that "are underpinned by differentiated analysis, supported by catalysts and sit at valuation levels that suggest upside."

The new names are: Nvidia (NVDA), Capital One Financial (COF), Expand Energy (EXE), Huntington Bancshares (HBAN), PVH (PVH) and UGI (UGI). ConocoPhillips (COP) and Global-E Online (GLBE) were removed.

Among other names are: Caterpillar (CAT), DraftKings (DKNG), Gilead (GILD), Microsoft (MSFT) and Ventas (VTR).

Check out the whole list.

data.title: PINFRA Has Appreciated As US-Mexico Trade Faces Great Challenges (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Since I recommended Promotora y Operadora de Infraestructura (OTCPK:PUODY) in October 2024, the stock is up 30%.

This article reviews the company’s 4Q24 and 1Q25 results. The results show a concerning deceleration of traffic in the northern highways, and the advancement in large projects for up to MXN 25 billion in investments.

The name now trades at about MXN 80 billion, compared with a net income capacity of MXN 6/7 billion per year.

I believe the undervaluation of the asset has shrunk, and therefore move it to Hold.

Recent Results

Highway traffic challenges: The main driver of current business at PINFRA is traffic. Here, we need to note a challenging dynamic. 4Q24 saw traffic grow 4% YoY, but this slowed to just 0.5% on average in 1Q25.

The situation becomes more concerning when we see the specifics. The highway falling the most, by far, is Monterrey–Nuevo Laredo, which connects the northern industrial hub of Mexico with Texas, as seen in the table below.

This is indicative of weakening conditions at the border and a slowdown in bilateral trade between the US and Mexico. This is concerning because Mexico’s economy is heavily reliant on U.S. trade, and further declines could start to affect more central highways like Ecatepec or Pacific routes like Colima.

No additions to projects: Besides the already announced developments currently under execution, there were no new project announcements in either 4Q24 or 1Q25. This isn’t necessarily negative, as the company already has MXN 25 billion in projects underway (more on this below).

Changes in revenue are irrelevant: Reported revenue rose 20% in 4Q24 and then dropped 5% in 1Q25, but these changes were entirely driven by fluctuations in construction activity.

The construction segment generates zero profits because it is simply an accounting segment. Its purpose is to build up the intangible assets behind each concession, which are then amortized over the life of the toll road.

Recapping the business model

PUODY's business is simple. The company builds a highway, and instead of charging the federal or provincial state, it capitalizes the cost as an intangible asset, because it gains the right to recover that expense plus a return over the life of the highway concession.

So far, the net return on those intangible assets has been around 15–20%, depending on utilization. This can be approximated by looking at operating income over intangibles below. From that figure, we need to add back the portion of intangibles that have already been depreciated (approximately 50%), and also remove taxes (~30%) to arrive at a net return on gross investments.

With current operations, the company can generate net income of MXN 6–7 billion per year after taxes and distributions to minority interests on a gross intangible base of MXN 56 billion.

In addition, the company is investing approximately MXN 25 billion into new highways, which are expected to begin operations between 2H25 and 2H27. Assuming these concessions deliver similar ROAs to past projects, they could add MXN 3.5 billion in net income once stabilized, although this is speculative.

In total, this would bring annual net income to the MXN 9–10 billion range, and FCF (before investment in new highways) to roughly MXN 1 billion higher than that. The company does not currently return much to shareholders via dividends or buybacks. Most cash is reinvested into new concessions.

Valuation

Today, PINFRA trades at a market cap of MXN 80 billion, or 12x on current earnings. Assuming earnings grow 50% over the next four years from the new concessions and ~5% per year organically from traffic and inflation (compounded), we could be looking at total growth of ~15% per year. This is the only source of return, as profits from current periods are reinvested in growth.

If the terminal multiple contracts to 9x, then about 5–6% of those returns are offset. The net result is a ~10% expected return, down from the ~15–20% I estimated at the time of my last recommendation. This makes sense given the higher stock price.

PUODY remains a high-quality infrastructure asset. The economics are stable, the margins are excellent, and the balance sheet is conservative. However, the appreciation in price has removed much of the attractiveness of the name. I now believe the name is fairly valued.

A potential return to more attractive levels could happen if U.S.–Mexico trade slows further, affecting traffic, earnings, and valuation. For now, I move PUODY to Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Exxon Mobil Corp: Substantial Value
data.description:
Oil prices for WTI have slid towards $60/barrel as of late which is poised to pressure the profitability of all majors like Exxon Mobil Corp. (NYSE:XOM) and other crude oil-focused energy companies.

Exxon Mobil Corp., however, was widely profitable in the first quarter and the company should have no problems covering its earnings with income generated from its oil and gas operations.

My last stop classification on Exxon Mobil Corp. was ‘Strong Buy’ and I made a bullish call on XOM primarily because the growing supply of data centers suggested that energy demand was poised to accelerate, therefore setting up a favorable investment situation for the energy industry.

Exxon Mobil Corp. is a solid investment, manages to grow its earnings even in a lower-price environment and will continue to raise its dividend moving forward.

Long-Term Energy Growth Will Keep Exxon Mobil Corp. Relevant For Many Years

Despite all the talk about depleting fossil fuel reserves, crude oil will remain an important energy pillar for years, if not decades, to come. Based on data from Exxon Mobil Corp., oil and natural gas are anticipated to account for more than half of the entire energy supply even in 2050.

Based on 2023 supply data, also provided by Exxon Mobil Corp., crude oil and natural gas accounted for 56% of the total energy supply, meaning despite billions of dollars invested in measures to mitigate the harmful effects of climate change, crude oil and natural gas are going to remain cornerstone energy sources for companies to serve their consumer markets.

For Exxon Mobil Corp. this means that the energy behemoth is, despite concerns about greenhouse gas emissions, going to stay relevant as a major domestic energy supplier for decades to come and the company’s profitability is thus poised to grow for a long time.

Exxon Mobil Corp.’s 1Q25 earnings presentation showed that its upstream segment is incredibly profitable and still accounts for the overwhelming majority (88%) of its total earnings.

Though the energy company has seen about 12% lower price realizations in 1Q25 compared to last year, in both U.S. and non-U.S. production, upstream-related earnings increased by $1.1 billion YoY.

This growth was contributed partially by growth in Exxon Mobil Corp.’s advantaged portfolio which includes operations in the Permian. As a matter of fact, production growth has been the main catalyst for Exxon Mobil Corp.’s profit surge in 1Q25, accounting for $920 million of the $1.1 billion profit growth realized in the last quarter.

Exxon Mobil Corp. has doubled down on its advantaged portfolio and, as a consequence, has enjoyed a substantial increase in production that was more than sufficient to compensate for lower realized crude oil prices. Long-term, Exxon Mobil Corp. plans on recognizing at least $20 billion in additional profits until 2030, compared to 2024, mainly due to investments in production capacity in the advantaged portfolio.

Moreover, it is not only growth in the advanced portfolio that is making positive contributions to the energy company’s bottom line. Exxon Mobil Corp.’s management put forth an ambitious cost optimization plan in December 2024 that calls for another $6 billion in structural cost savings by the end of the decade, on top of the $12.1 billion that have already been realized since 2019.

Exxon Mobil Corp Has Substantial Earnings Value For Investors

Exxon Mobil Corp. is presently valued at a 13.1 times 2026e profit multiple which implies a 20% YoY profit jump. Thus, Exxon Mobil Corp. has the second-highest Price-To-Earnings ratio just behind Chevron Corp. (CVX).

Chevron has about half the market value than Exxon Mobil Corp. but the company is also solidly profitable: The major is selling for a Price-To-Earnings ratio of 14.2x and Chevron is anticipated to grow its profits 24% YoY in 2026, the fastest in the peer group.

In my view, Exxon Mobil Corp.’s stock could re-rate to a 14-15x 2026e profit multiple which leads us to an implied intrinsic value of $109 to $117 per share. Obviously, this price target may only be realized if crude price don’t slide further and stabilize in the $60-70/barrel range.

ConocoPhillips (COP), an upstream-focused energy company, is selling for 12.1x estimated 2026 profits, but the energy company went full in on its upstream focus more than a decade ago when it spun off its downstream business.

From a yield angle, Chevron presently offers investors the highest dividend yield of 5% which is about 20% above the peer group average of 4.2%. Exxon Mobil Corp. still offers a highly competitive 3.9% and the company is poised to grow its pay-out moving forward, thanks to the upstream business propping up the company’s profit growth.

Why The Investment Thesis Might Be More Bearish

The worst possible scenario for Exxon Mobil Corp. would probably a serious downturn in crude oil prices. Such as decline would only be likely to occur during a recession which is presently not on the horizon. As a matter of fact, inflation numbers showed that inflation cooled considerably in the last three months.

Taking into account that Exxon Mobil Corp. is heavily focused on production and is a globally diversified energy company, I think that a company of the size and scale like XOM has the best shot of riding out an economic downturn, should one occur in the next couple of years.

And since Exxon Mobil Corp. has been growing its dividend throughout a variety of different price environments, I anticipate Exxon Mobil Corp. To keep growing its dividend even if crude oil prices fall further.

My Takeaway

Despite growing pressure on crude oil prices, I think that Exxon Mobil Corp. could be a core holding for passive income investors. Taking into account Exxon Mobil Corp.’s considerable size and the company’s long history of paying dividends, I think it is going to continue to grow its dividend moving forward.

The lack of a major shift in the estimated energy mix long-term also strongly suggest that Exxon Mobil Corp. has intact long-term growth prospects and XOM’s advanced portfolio is going to be key here. Exxon Mobil Corp.’s Permian posture as well as its ability to grow its earnings despite lower price realizations underscores Exxon Mobil Corp.’s value.

Last but not least, Exxon Mobil Corp.’s stock is selling for a moderate Price-To-Earnings Ratio and offers passive income investors less downside compared to purely upstream-focused energy companies like ConocoPhillips.

data.title: US Bancorp: Improving Fundamentals With Reasonable Valuations
data.description:
Introduction

Minnesota-based US Bancorp (NYSE:USB) is a diversified financial services company with a strong banking presence in the Midwest and western region with ~3500 branches spread across 29 states. Apart from a strong consumer banking division, USB also has a distinct emphasis on its fee-based businesses that complement its core banking business. USB generates ~40% of its revenue from fee-based business and the rest from traditional banking.

The recent quarterly results were quite good, with the company reporting improvement in the efficiency ratio as well as asset quality. Given a lucrative valuation and a potential for further efficiency improvement, strengthen the case for a Buy rating on USB.

Improving performance

Back in April, USB came out with upbeat results, marking its fourth consecutive quarterly beat on the bottom-line front and first on the top-line front. The beat came in a rather challenging macroeconomic environment and uncertainty regarding the tariffs. The company reported $1.03 Non-GAAP EPS, beating the consensus estimates by $0.05, driven by better NIMs and improving efficiency ratios, while the revenue came in at $6.96 billion, beating the consensus by ~$50 million.

USB has delivered on most of the metrics in the latest quarter with good revenue growth, better efficiency metrics and an improving asset quality. Moreover, the management has reaffirmed their earlier guidance for 2025 despite the challenging macroeconomic environment.

The company reported Net interest income slightly higher than $4.1 billion, a ~1.3% decline sequentially and a 2.7% growth YoY. On a more positive note, the non-interest income came in slightly higher than $2.8 billion, flat sequentially but growing ~5% YoY. The faster fee income growth is partly attributed to management’s strategic focus on the growth of fee income segments, as it also serves as the starting point for client engagement for the traditional banking business. Within the noninterest income category Payment services and Trust & Investment Management subsegments delivered good growth, driving the overall noninterest income.

Coming further down the line, the company reported quite a good efficiency improvement, with the operating leverage trending positive for the third consecutive quarter. Moreover, the efficiency ratio has also improved quite a lot, from 66.4% in 1Q24 to ~60.8% in the latest quarter. Adjusting for some one-off expenses like merger and integration expenses originating from the acquisition of MUFG Union bank, the expense ratio for 1Q24 was ~62.5%. Still, the current quarter’s 60.8% efficiency ratio is quite a notable improvement even after adjustments.

Meanwhile, the company’s net interest margins (NIMs) also improved, backed by an improving efficiency ratio as well as fixed asset repricing from the rate cuts. While the rate cut benefits are temporary in nature, the improvement in NIMs from improvement in efficiency ratio is likely to be sticky. Management has intended to further improve its efficiency ratio and is targeting a mid-50s range for this ratio. Moreover, for NIMs, which currently stands at 2.72%, management has been targeting 3% until 2026-2027. Given the improvement achieved over the last year, I believe the targets are quite achievable now.

Over the last 3 quarters, USB has reported a consecutive decline in allowance for credit losses as a percentage of total loans. Currently, the ratio stands at 2.07%, which is a 2 bps improvement over 4Q24 and a 5 bps improvement over 3Q24. On a nominal basis also, the allowance for credit losses has declined by $23 million to $537 million. The declining allowance for credit losses is a positive sign for USB, as it signals management’s confidence in the current state of the company’s asset quality.

Moreover, other credit metrics are also signaling a positive direction for asset quality. For instance, USB’s majority of the subsegment delinquency rate in the early bucket category (30-89 days delinquency bucket) has been trending better QoQ except for the other retail segment, which has witnessed a brief deterioration in this asset quality metric; however, given its small proportion to USB’s loan book, it wouldn’t be much of a concern.

Valuation and Outlook

As per Seeking Alpha's quant valuation rating, USB is rated a B, which is a decent rating. While the rating system analyzes a range of valuation ratios to arrive at this rating, I try to value lending businesses through the price-to-book (P/B) ratio viewed in conjunction with the sustainable return on equity (ROE) ratio.

USB is currently trading at ~1.2x P/B (forward), which is a decent discount to its 5-year average of ~1.5x. USB’s ROE currently stands at 12.3%, which is in line with the 5-year average. This gives a yield (ROE divided by P/B ratio) of ~10.3% at current forward P/B and ROE.

Moreover, given stable and improving asset quality, projected efficiency improvements, and faster fee income growth, I believe USB’s ROE should improve going forward. As per USB management’s guidance, they are targeting an ROE of high teens, which is significantly higher than the current ROE number. Therefore, it wouldn’t be an understatement that the current yield has an upside potential from current ~10.3% levels. If we assume an ROE of ~15-16% (mid-teens), the yield comes out to be 12.5-13.3%, which, I believe, is quite achievable.

Macroeconomic uncertainty a key risk

The primary risk regarding USB and banking stocks in general is the macroeconomic uncertainty. Although tariff-related uncertainty is quite lower now as compared to early April. However, these uncertainties can resurface in the near future, as the 3-month window to negotiate a trade deal with the US is coming to an end for many trading partners. If these trading partners fail to negotiate a deal with the US, there could be a situation where the US could reimpose tariffs proposed earlier on its counterparts. These tariffs would hurt domestic consumers and result in higher raw material costs for domestic manufacturing. Moreover, retaliatory tariffs from the counterparts could further exacerbate the situation, leading to a slower growth outlook.

Conclusion

Given stable asset quality as well as the prospect of improvement of the efficiency ratio combined with lucrative valuation, I believe USB is a good buy at the current valuation.

data.title: Air Taxis And Oil Money Mix Well: Initiating Joby Aviation With A Buy
data.description:
Joby Aviation, Inc. (NYSE:JOBY) is not a mainstream name, and that’s because it's working in what is today still a niche market: developing EV takeoff and landing aircraft (eVTOL) with the goal of coming out with an aerial ridesharing service. The company has been around since 2009, but only went public in 2021, since the stock has gone down 25% from $10.71 per share to around $7.88 per share at current levels. The stock is up almost 24% over the past month after signing a memorandum of understanding (MoU) with Abdul Latif Jameel (an eight-decades-old network of diversified businesses) to “explore opportunities to establish a distribution agreement in Saudi Arabia for Joby’s electric aircraft.” We're initiating the stock with a Buy not on this deal, but on the potential growth Joby's network can reel in once aVTOL hits its inflection point.

Joby's talks with Saudi Arabia follow the trend of U.S.-backed companies getting cozy with the oil-rich nation. Nothing is set in stone yet, but the potential is to deliver “up to 200 Joby aircraft and related services valued at approximately $1B are expected by businesses over the coming years.” So, the stock is trading up on the fact that a deal may be coming soon, and if so, it would dramatically reverse Joby's financials, which, as of Q1, still have no revenue to show, as seen below. Not to mention, it would also boost the stock price, which increased roughly 28% between Tuesday and Wednesday of the last week on the Toyota (TM) investment alone and is still digesting the potential of deals with Saudi Arabia.

Joby's in bed with all the right people

For now, we remain in the preliminary phase of this deal and of the broader air taxi sector. But this is a window to buy in on what we believe to be an extremely uniquely-positioned company with clean execution. What’s holding investor confidence together on this deal is the fact that the Saudi investor group already invests in Joby and has close ties with Toyota.

Why does this matter? Toyota Motor Corp is a top shareholder in Joby and announced the investment of $250M in Joby in late May, marking the first half of a $500M commitment. As of Q1, reported in early May, Joby has around $813M in cash and short-term investments, and then the $500M from Toyota on top of that. So, Joby is pretty well-positioned when it comes to reach within the mobility market, which should help secure its go-to-market strategy. This is a big positive because when it comes to new tech, the real struggle is getting commercial adoption and that's what Joby is working on very early on.

So, what we know so far is that Joby is working on getting U.S. Federal Aviation Administration certification, expected in 2H25, and is likely to launch its first commercial flights in the Middle East, but that doesn’t mean it’s ignoring American markets. The company is also partnering with Delta Air Lines (DAL) and Uber (UBER) to prepare the U.S. market for launch, eyeing Los Angeles and New York City as the main launch markets.

We see more attractive entry points at current levels, considering the stock price is erasing some of its gains after the news. The RSI bumped into overbought territory in late May on the Toyota investment before falling to 60, and the stock is up 24% over the past three months, as shown on the chart below against the S&P 500 (SP500). Due to all this momentum, it is no surprise that Joby trades at premium multiples; its Price to Book is 7.27x compared to a group average of 2.90x. Its forward EV/Sales, based on Seeking Alpha’s metrics, is also insane at 2,965.41x compared to a group average of 1.93x. So, is it the right time to buy in?

The thing is, Joby has a strong standing within the very new sector of eVTOL and urban air mobility, estimated to grow at a CAGR of 35.6% between 2024-2028, but this doesn't mean it won't experience hurdles. So while we think this is an attractive long-term pick, we have two specific hurdles we're watching: regulations and commercial adoption. These are two challenges Joby is working on, but that in our opinion investors should be well aware of heading into the stock.

Regulations:

The company is actively working on getting the necessary certification for flight operations from the FAA; on this quarter’s earnings call, management noted that:

I’m pleased to say that despite it being a shorter quarter, we actually beat last quarter’s record, accelerating forward by a full 12 percentage points on the FAA side and moving the Joby side up to 62% complete. This is critically important progress because each plan we sign off with the FAA is a step towards unlocking TIA flights.

The thing investors should keep in mind is that Joby is not the only one working on this. There’s a push from air taxi companies to get approval for air taxis as urban transportation demand expands and hits commercial markets. Vertical Aerospace (EVTL) is one of the other players working on this with its VX4 air taxi. Another is Archer Aviation (ACHR), and the other is Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF), which is extremely well-positioned in the Chinese market, with Bloomberg reporting "China is considering placing an order for hundreds of Airbus SE aircraft as soon as next month." While each of these players is approaching the market from a specific vertical, we think the global market is beginning to get segmented and we're glad Joby is calling dibs on the Middle East.

Joby is still in the testing phase now; it's testing all-electric aircraft that can carry up to four passengers and are constructed for urban air mobility, running up to 200 miles per hour. Earlier in May, the company announced “successfully completing flights with a full transition from vertical to cruise flight, and back again, with a pilot onboard.” Following that, the company announced the success of two simultaneous aircraft during testing for the first time.

More successful testing will ease the road to getting approval; CEO JoeBen Bevirt said that “with six aircraft in our test fleet, flying multiple aircraft at a time will play an important role in accelerating our certification testing and supporting our plans to carry our first passengers next year.” So, Joby is taking all the necessary steps to get the necessary certification, as shown in the roadmap from Q1 below, which would then leave it with its next big challenge: building up for commercial adoption.

Commercial adoption:

From current visibility, it looks like Joby is among the closest to actually seeing its air taxis take off commercially, but closest doesn’t mean close. We think that the market could be getting a bit ahead of itself as there are a lot of factors to take into account for this tech to work, and specifically to work in the climate of Saudi Arabia and/or Dubai, which are heavily prone to sandstorms and high heat levels. The company is eyeing Dubai in addition to Saudi Arabia with intentions to start passenger services by 2026, which is soon; the company’s shareholder letter highlighted that:

IN DUBAI, construction of the vertiport at the International Airport is progressing well and we remain on track to begin pilot-on-board testing in the Emirate mid-year, following the success of our inhabited transition flights in California.

While we do think special precautions need to be taken when testing happens in the Middle East, we don’t think it’s a task Joby can’t handle. The UAE is prepared to help, too; according to UAE civil aviation authority’s issued statement in February, the country is starting to map air corridors for air taxis, noting that "Air corridor mapping for piloted and autonomous air taxis and drones is a crucial milestone that will enable the seamless implementation of advanced air mobility into the UAE's infrastructure."

Joby has already applied for certification that would allow it to operate within the commercial air transport in the UAE since September of last year. This will be a slow-burning candle, with the mapping requiring an estimated 20 months to be defined, but the good news is that the ball is rolling. So, while we do think hurdles and sandstorms and competition may arise, we believe the longer-term runway for Joby’s position within the Middle East is secure.

FYI: The aircraft themselves are produced in Ohio and can deliver up to 500 aircraft per year; this makes Joby’s job of navigating potential tariffs easier, considering the company’s strong commitment to being part of the American innovation force. Bevirt noted that

“Our air taxi is designed, manufactured, and assembled here in the US, and we are a proudly American company, employing engineers and other experts across 40 different US states.”

This is good news because it solidifies the fact that Joby has a strong standing in the U.S., which could help when it comes to military adoption of their aircraft, which we think is an easier market to tap into than urban transportation, considering the higher defense spending coming from the U.S. and NATO.

Bottom Line: There is no revenue yet, meaning it won't be hard to increase $0 sales

Joby is still very much in its R&D phase of existence, so is the entire sector, for that matter. If you take a look at their financials for Q1, this becomes painfully clear. For Q1, Joby’s net loss was $82.4M with $163.3M in net operating loss and $80.9M in other income. The loss is shrinking, but investors shouldn't hold onto that too closely, as higher spend is needed for the next phase. What are now preliminary talks could very likely lead to a monetizable opportunity.

The stock is pricey and has run up on investor confidence in what deals may come with Saudi Arabia. While that is usually a turn-off for us, we think Joby is an exception because it's playing the long game and building up a first-mover advantage in what should be a multi-billion-dollar market.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Meta: AI Agents May Prove To Be A Multi-Billion-Dollar Opportunity
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (NASDAQ:META) has been my favorite large-cap tech stock in the past few years. Every time the stock plummeted - think of 2018, 2020, and 2022 - I have been brave enough to add to my stake, and I couldn't have been happier with that. Even during periods when Meta seemingly made miscalculated bets, which included doubling down on the metaverse, I had faith in the core business of the company. Today, investors are closely monitoring Meta's AI strategy, given how the company has ramped up capex to pursue a future as an AI-focused tech giant. Upon closer evaluation of Meta's spending on AI agents and the long-term monetization potential of these agents, I am convinced that AI agents present a multi-billion-dollar opportunity for the company.

Meta's AI Agents Are Already Available For Testing

Meta has developed AI agents across a few broad categories. At a broad level, these agents fall into one of the following categories.

  1. Conversational AI assistants
  2. Business AI agents
  3. Creator AI agents
  4. Specialized research AI agents

For the purpose of this analysis, I will only focus on Business AI agents as I believe this is where the biggest monetization opportunity lies, at least in the foreseeable future.

The core functionality of business AI agents is to help businesses interact with customers, automate certain processes, and assist with sales. Some of the key capabilities of these AI agents include responding to customer queries, generating sales leads, and automating ad creation. Meta has already started beta testing some of these features through its business AI bot, which is focused on supporting SMBs in delivering automated customer care. Announcing the pilot testing of business AI on LinkedIn last March, Meta's Head of Business AI Clara Shih wrote:

Today, I’m excited to share a sneak peek of Meta’s work on business AI– agents made easy for every business. Using the brand content you've shared on Instagram, Facebook, and WhatsApp, product catalogs, FAQs, and more, you can easily set up business AIs to respond instantly to customers, free your team from repetitive tasks, and help convert more leads, faster. Just as we did for ads, we want to simplify and democratize powerful AI tools for even the smallest of businesses.

For now, interested business users have to be on a waitlist to gain access to business AI agents developed by Meta. However, the company has pledged to bring these tools to all business users in the future.

2025 May Be The Year Of AI Agents

Meta is not the only company that believes in the massive potential of AI agents. Earlier this year, in a blog post, OpenAI's Sam Altman made a strong case for 2025 being the year in which AI agents come to the spotlight. He wrote:

We believe that, in 2025, we may see the first AI agents “join the workforce” and materially change the output of companies. We continue to believe that iteratively putting great tools in the hands of people leads to great, broadly-distributed outcomes.

Giving a simplified example focused on the introduction of a software engineer AI agent, Sam Altman wrote:

Let’s imagine the case of a software engineering agent, which is an agent that we expect to be particularly important. Imagine that this agent will eventually be capable of doing most things a software engineer at a top company with a few years of experience could do, for tasks up to a couple of days long. It will not have the biggest new ideas, it will require lots of human supervision and direction, and it will be great at some things but surprisingly bad at others. Still, imagine it as a real-but-relatively-junior virtual coworker. Now imagine 1,000 of them. Or 1 million of them. Now imagine such agents in every field of knowledge work.

Meta, which is powering its AI agents with Llama, will be up against the likes of ChatGPT. OpenAI has already explored the idea of AI agents through the Assistants API, which is a tool developed by the AI giant to help product developers utilize its technology to build custom AI assistants that can function as agents. According to The Information, OpenAI is planning to charge close to $20,000 a month for advanced research agents and approximately $2,000 a month for software developer AI agents.

After comparing the Assistants API with Meta's business AI, I believe these tools cater to different end markets.

OpenAI's Assistants API is more focused on businesses that are focused on creating deeply integrated AI agents that are not limited to just a single platform. Meta, on the other hand, targets SMBs that already have a meaningful presence on its platforms (Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp). In this context, Meta is building integrated AI agents that can be easily deployed into the existing ecosystem of a business whereas OpenAI primarily functions as a technology solutions provider for product developers.

Projecting Direct Revenues For Business AI

To get a ballpark idea of the revenue opportunity we are looking at, I did some calculations using conservative assumptions and publicly available data. CEO Mark Zuckerberg has already hinted at monetization in several instances, so it makes sense for investors to take AI agents seriously, especially the business AI product suite. After announcing that Meta AI surpassed one billion monthly active users a few days ago, Zuckerberg commented:

There will be opportunities to either insert paid recommendations or offer a subscription service so that people can pay to use more compute.

For business AI tools, I believe Meta is likely to focus on a subscription-based pricing model although paid recommendations could be a consideration in the early stages. Monetization for business AI will, in my opinion, involve two distinct product categories.

  1. Business messaging monetization, which includes business customers on WhatsApp Business, Facebook Messenger, and Instagram DM.
  2. AI ad automation monetization.

Let's first look at the addressable target market for Meta business AI.

According to Statista, WhatsApp Business had almost 765 million monthly active users as of Q4 2024. The number of active WhatsApp Business users has grown exponentially since 2020.

Exhibit 1: WhatsApp Business MAUs

This, unfortunately, is not an accurate representation of the number of actual businesses that use the platform. I use WhatsApp Business as well, but only because I have two mobile numbers. Since WhatsApp does not allow using two numbers on one device, the easy workaround is to download and use WhatsApp Business for your second number. Based on mid-2024 statistics, around 50 million businesses used the platform at the time. For my projections, I will use this as a baseline.

Facebook Messenger has over a billion MAUs, but the absolute majority of these users are individuals, not businesses. According to some recent estimates, around 40 million businesses use Facebook Messenger monthly. This accounts for around 4% of MAUs and is a more realistic estimate of monthly business users.

Instagram, on the other hand, has over two billion MAUs, and approximately 10% of these are businesses, so we are looking at around 200 million global business users. The rise of influencer marketing in the past decade has forced businesses of every size and scale to join and maintain business profiles on Instagram to reach the younger demographic.

When it comes to AI ad automation, I am focused on Meta's plans to fully automate the ad creation process by the end of 2026. According to Meta's projections, businesses will soon be able to handle the entire ad campaign, from inputting product images to targeting the right demographic market, using AI. In my opinion, Meta will be in a good position to charge a subscription fee from advertisers for these features.

In order to get an idea about the prices Meta would be able to charge businesses for all these AI agents, I looked at the existing pricing models of AI SaaS tools.

  • Salesforce Einstein starts from $75/month.
  • HubSpot AI starts from $20 per user a month.
  • Jasper, a popular content creation tool, starts from $39/month.
  • Popular chatbots developed by Chatfuel, Botsonic, and Tidio start from around $20/month.
  • Currently available lead generation tools such as Apollo and Outreach start from around $49 per user a month.

The next step is to project potential revenues for Meta.

It is important to note that Meta's business AI agents are likely to be able to charge premium prices if these tools prove to be game-changing, enabling businesses to replace agency work with AI agents. However, to err on the side of caution, I have decided to use more conservative assumptions for pricing and adoption to get a general idea of the revenue potential of AI agents.

In a simplified scenario, I believe Meta's target market will be around 110 million businesses for its business AI suite. This includes 40 million WhatsApp Business users, 50 million businesses that use Messenger, and 20 million Instagram business accounts (just 10% of all business accounts). In the initial phase, I expect Meta to capture a 15% share of this target market for its business AI suite which includes AI business messaging features. This gives us 16.5 million paying subscribers.

At an average annual revenue per business of $700, we are looking at $11.5 billion in annual revenue.

The AI ad automation tool is likely to enjoy much higher average annual revenue per business, but at a lower adoption rate in my opinion. Assuming average annual revenue of $1,500 and an adoption rate of just 5% (5.5 million businesses), projected revenue comes to $8.25 billion.

In total, therefore, we are looking at almost a $20 billion revenue opportunity with AI agents in the initial phase of monetization.

While I used a highly simplified scenario, rumored pricing strategies for OpenAI's AI agents suggest Meta may actually end up bringing in a substantially higher revenue from this segment in the long run.

Risks To Monitor

When it comes to AI agents, I believe the biggest risk is the threat of competitors. I will keep a close eye on established AI giants such as OpenAI and Anthropic to identify any adverse movements in the competitive landscape.

In general, investing in Meta carries several other risks, including unfavorable regulatory actions and the possibility that ramped-up capital expenditures on AI deliver lackluster returns in the short run. In addition, given that Meta relies on advertising revenue, the advertising market's sensitivity to global economic growth also poses a risk. Given rising geopolitical tensions and uncertain tax policies, investors should not rule out the possibility of a major slowdown in the global economy. This would negatively impact the global advertising industry.

Takeaway

Meta Platforms is well-positioned to emerge as a big winner of the growing popularity of AI agents. The company is already exploring with a few agents, and there is massive monetization potential when it comes to its business AI suite. Starting next year, I believe we will see Meta focusing on the monetization of its AI product suite given that monthly users have now surpassed one billion (this fits well into Meta's historical playbook). Although Meta may not be cheaply valued at a forward P/E of 26, I continue to believe in the company's potential to grow in double-digits in the next five years.
